title: Real Property
subject: Real Property
summary: Real Property is about who owns land, who can use land, who can transfer land, and what happens when land is rented, sold, mortgaged, restricted, or used by others.

sections.title: Big Picture
body: Real Property is about who owns land, who can use land, who can transfer land, and what happens when land is rented, sold, mortgaged, restricted, or used by others.
The main MBE question is usually:
Who has the better right to the land or to use the land?
Fixtures, crops, zoning, water, support, and air rights

blocks.text: Real Property is about who owns land, who can use land, who can transfer land, and what happens when land is rented, sold, mortgaged, restricted, or used by others.
The main MBE question is usually:
Who has the better right to the land or to use the land?
Fixtures, crops, zoning, water, support, and air rights

sections.title: 1. What Is Real Property?
body: A garage attached to the land
An easement to cross someone else’s land
Real property = land + attached things + land rights.

blocks.text: A garage attached to the land
An easement to cross someone else’s land
Real property = land + attached things + land rights.

sections.title: 2. Present Estates
body: A present estate means someone has the right to possess the property now.

blocks.text: A present estate means someone has the right to possess the property now.

sections.title: 3. Fee Simple Absolute
body: A fee simple absolute is the broadest ownership interest.
Full ownership that can last forever.
Jordan has a fee simple absolute.

blocks.text: A fee simple absolute is the broadest ownership interest.
Full ownership that can last forever.
Jordan has a fee simple absolute.

sections.title: 4. Defeasible Fees
body: A defeasible fee is ownership that can end if a stated event happens.
Does the deed give the land forever, or does it attach a real condition?
If the deed only states a purpose, it is usually not defeasible.
“To Jordan for use as a garden.”
That usually gives Jordan a fee simple absolute.
It states the purpose, but it does not clearly say Jordan loses the land if it is not used as a garden.

blocks.text: A defeasible fee is ownership that can end if a stated event happens.
Does the deed give the land forever, or does it attach a real condition?
If the deed only states a purpose, it is usually not defeasible.
“To Jordan for use as a garden.”
That usually gives Jordan a fee simple absolute.
It states the purpose, but it does not clearly say Jordan loses the land if it is not used as a garden.

sections.title: 5. Fee Simple Determinable
body: A fee simple determinable automatically ends when the condition happens.
“To Jordan so long as the land is used as a community garden.”
If the land stops being used as a community garden, it automatically goes back to Riley.

blocks.text: A fee simple determinable automatically ends when the condition happens.
“To Jordan so long as the land is used as a community garden.”
If the land stops being used as a community garden, it automatically goes back to Riley.

sections.title: 6. Fee Simple Subject to Condition Subsequent
body: A fee simple subject to condition subsequent does not end automatically.
The grantor must take action to retake the land.
Right of entry / right of reentry / power of termination
“To Jordan, but if Jordan uses the land for commercial storage, Riley may reenter and retake the land.”
If Jordan uses it for commercial storage, Jordan does not automatically lose the land.
Riley must act to retake it.
Condition subsequent = grantor must do something.

blocks.text: A fee simple subject to condition subsequent does not end automatically.
The grantor must take action to retake the land.
Right of entry / right of reentry / power of termination
“To Jordan, but if Jordan uses the land for commercial storage, Riley may reenter and retake the land.”
If Jordan uses it for commercial storage, Jordan does not automatically lose the land.
Riley must act to retake it.
Condition subsequent = grantor must do something.

sections.title: 7. Fee Simple Subject to Executory Interest
body: A fee simple subject to executory interest ends automatically when the condition happens, but the land goes to a third party, not back to the grantor.
“To Jordan, but if Jordan stops using the land as a garden, then to Casey.”
If Jordan stops using it as a garden, the land automatically goes to Casey.
Executory interest = third party gets it.

blocks.text: A fee simple subject to executory interest ends automatically when the condition happens, but the land goes to a third party, not back to the grantor.
“To Jordan, but if Jordan stops using the land as a garden, then to Casey.”
If Jordan stops using it as a garden, the land automatically goes to Casey.
Executory interest = third party gets it.

sections.title: 8. Defeasible Fee Quick Chart
body: Estate — Key Language — What Happens? — Future Interest
Fee simple determinable — so long as, until, while — Ends automatically — Possibility of reverter
Fee simple subject to condition subsequent — but if, provided that, on condition that — Grantor must act — Right of reentry
Fee simple subject to executory interest — condition + then to third party — Third party gets it automatically — Executory interest

blocks.text: Estate — Key Language — What Happens? — Future Interest
Fee simple determinable — so long as, until, while — Ends automatically — Possibility of reverter
Fee simple subject to condition subsequent — but if, provided that, on condition that — Grantor must act — Right of reentry
Fee simple subject to executory interest — condition + then to third party — Third party gets it automatically — Executory interest

sections.title: 9. Fee Tail
body: A fee tail tries to keep land in a family bloodline.
“To Jordan and the heirs of Jordan’s body.”
Most states have abolished the fee tail.
Usually, an attempted fee tail becomes a fee simple absolute.
Fee tail = old family-line estate, mostly abolished.

blocks.text: A fee tail tries to keep land in a family bloodline.
“To Jordan and the heirs of Jordan’s body.”
Most states have abolished the fee tail.
Usually, an attempted fee tail becomes a fee simple absolute.
Fee tail = old family-line estate, mostly abolished.

sections.title: 10. Life Estate
body: A life estate lasts for someone’s life.
“To Jordan for life, then to Casey.”
When Jordan dies, Casey gets possession.
Life estate = ownership for a lifetime.

blocks.text: A life estate lasts for someone’s life.
“To Jordan for life, then to Casey.”
When Jordan dies, Casey gets possession.
Life estate = ownership for a lifetime.

sections.title: 11. Life Estate Pur Autre Vie
body: A life estate pur autre vie is a life estate measured by someone else’s life.
Jordan can possess the land until Casey dies.
Pur autre vie = for another person’s life.

blocks.text: A life estate pur autre vie is a life estate measured by someone else’s life.
Jordan can possess the land until Casey dies.
Pur autre vie = for another person’s life.

sections.title: 12. Future Interests After a Life Estate
body: If the Future Interest Goes Back to the Grantor
When Jordan dies, the land goes back to Riley.
If the Future Interest Goes to a Third Party
“To Jordan for life, then to Casey.”
Back to grantor = reversion. To third party after life estate = remainder.

blocks.text: If the Future Interest Goes Back to the Grantor
When Jordan dies, the land goes back to Riley.
If the Future Interest Goes to a Third Party
“To Jordan for life, then to Casey.”
Back to grantor = reversion. To third party after life estate = remainder.

sections.title: 13. Life Tenant’s Rights
body: Collect rent during the life estate
But the life tenant cannot destroy the future interest holder’s value.
Jordan has a life estate in a house.
Jordan can rent the house to a tenant.
But Jordan cannot tear the house down just because Jordan wants the bricks.
Life tenant can use the property, but cannot ruin it for the future owner.

blocks.text: Collect rent during the life estate
But the life tenant cannot destroy the future interest holder’s value.
Jordan has a life estate in a house.
Jordan can rent the house to a tenant.
But Jordan cannot tear the house down just because Jordan wants the bricks.
Life tenant can use the property, but cannot ruin it for the future owner.

sections.title: 14. Waste
body: Waste limits what a life tenant or tenant can do to property.
The life tenant must generally return the property in substantially the same condition, allowing normal wear and tear.

blocks.text: Waste limits what a life tenant or tenant can do to property.
The life tenant must generally return the property in substantially the same condition, allowing normal wear and tear.

sections.title: 15. Permissive Waste
body: The life tenant lets the property fall apart.
Jordan has a life estate in a townhouse.
The life tenant must make reasonable repairs, usually limited by the benefit the life tenant receives from the property.
Permissive waste = letting the property decay.

blocks.text: The life tenant lets the property fall apart.
Jordan has a life estate in a townhouse.
The life tenant must make reasonable repairs, usually limited by the benefit the life tenant receives from the property.
Permissive waste = letting the property decay.

sections.title: 16. Voluntary Waste
body: Voluntary waste means the life tenant actively damages or changes the property in a harmful way.
Jordan has a life estate in wooded land.
Jordan cuts down all the trees and sells the lumber, even though no such cutting was allowed.
Voluntary waste = actively damaging the property.

blocks.text: Voluntary waste means the life tenant actively damages or changes the property in a harmful way.
Jordan has a life estate in wooded land.
Jordan cuts down all the trees and sells the lumber, even though no such cutting was allowed.
Voluntary waste = actively damaging the property.

sections.title: 17. Ameliorative Waste
body: Ameliorative waste means the life tenant changes the property in a way that improves value but still changes the property.
Jordan has a life estate in an old single-family home.
The neighborhood becomes mostly apartment buildings.
Jordan converts the home into apartments, increasing the value.
Modern courts often allow this if reasonable.
Ameliorative waste = valuable improvement, but still a major change.

blocks.text: Ameliorative waste means the life tenant changes the property in a way that improves value but still changes the property.
Jordan has a life estate in an old single-family home.
The neighborhood becomes mostly apartment buildings.
Jordan converts the home into apartments, increasing the value.
Modern courts often allow this if reasonable.
Ameliorative waste = valuable improvement, but still a major change.

sections.title: 18. Future Interests
body: A future interest is ownership that may become possessory later.

blocks.text: A future interest is ownership that may become possessory later.

sections.title: 19. Future Interests in the Grantor
body: A reversion follows a life estate when the grantor does not give the future interest to someone else.
A possibility of reverter follows a fee simple determinable.
A right of reentry follows a fee simple subject to condition subsequent.
Grantor future interests = reversion, possibility of reverter, right of reentry.

blocks.text: A reversion follows a life estate when the grantor does not give the future interest to someone else.
A possibility of reverter follows a fee simple determinable.
A right of reentry follows a fee simple subject to condition subsequent.
Grantor future interests = reversion, possibility of reverter, right of reentry.

sections.title: 20. Remainders
body: A remainder is a future interest in a third party that becomes possessory naturally when the prior estate ends.
Usually, a remainder follows a life estate.
“To Jordan for life, then to Casey.”
Remainder waits patiently for the prior estate to end.

blocks.text: A remainder is a future interest in a third party that becomes possessory naturally when the prior estate ends.
Usually, a remainder follows a life estate.
“To Jordan for life, then to Casey.”
Remainder waits patiently for the prior estate to end.

sections.title: 21. Vested Remainder
body: A vested remainder is given to an identifiable person and has no condition precedent.
“To Jordan for life, then to Casey.”
Casey does not need to satisfy a condition.
Vested = known person, no condition before taking.

blocks.text: A vested remainder is given to an identifiable person and has no condition precedent.
“To Jordan for life, then to Casey.”
Casey does not need to satisfy a condition.
Vested = known person, no condition before taking.

sections.title: 22. Vested Remainder Subject to Open
body: A vested remainder subject to open exists when at least one class member is known, but more people can join the class later.
“To Jordan for life, then to Jordan’s children.”
Jordan has one child now but may have more.
That child has a vested remainder subject to open.
Subject to open = class can grow.

blocks.text: A vested remainder subject to open exists when at least one class member is known, but more people can join the class later.
“To Jordan for life, then to Jordan’s children.”
Jordan has one child now but may have more.
That child has a vested remainder subject to open.
Subject to open = class can grow.

sections.title: 23. Vested Remainder Subject to Complete Divestment
body: A vested remainder subject to complete divestment is a vested remainder that can be completely taken away by a later event.
“To Jordan for life, then to Casey, but if Casey is not living at Jordan’s death, then to Morgan.”
Casey has a vested remainder subject to complete divestment.
Vested now, but can be lost later.

blocks.text: A vested remainder subject to complete divestment is a vested remainder that can be completely taken away by a later event.
“To Jordan for life, then to Casey, but if Casey is not living at Jordan’s death, then to Morgan.”
Casey has a vested remainder subject to complete divestment.
Vested now, but can be lost later.

sections.title: 24. Contingent Remainder
body: The person is not yet identifiable; or
“To Jordan for life, then to Jordan’s grandchildren.”
The grandchildren have a contingent remainder.
“To Jordan for life, then to Casey if Casey becomes a lawyer.”
Casey has a contingent remainder because Casey must satisfy a condition first.

blocks.text: The person is not yet identifiable; or
“To Jordan for life, then to Jordan’s grandchildren.”
The grandchildren have a contingent remainder.
“To Jordan for life, then to Casey if Casey becomes a lawyer.”
Casey has a contingent remainder because Casey must satisfy a condition first.

sections.title: 25. Executory Interests
body: An executory interest is a future interest in a third party that usually cuts short someone else’s estate.

blocks.text: An executory interest is a future interest in a third party that usually cuts short someone else’s estate.

sections.title: 26. Shifting Executory Interest
body: A shifting executory interest cuts short another grantee’s estate.
“To Jordan, but if Casey graduates college, then to Casey.”
If Casey graduates, the land shifts from Jordan to Casey.

blocks.text: A shifting executory interest cuts short another grantee’s estate.
“To Jordan, but if Casey graduates college, then to Casey.”
If Casey graduates, the land shifts from Jordan to Casey.

sections.title: 27. Springing Executory Interest
body: A springing executory interest cuts short the grantor’s estate or fills a gap after the grantor gets possession.
Until Casey turns 25, Riley keeps the land.
When Casey turns 25, Casey’s interest springs out of Riley.

blocks.text: A springing executory interest cuts short the grantor’s estate or fills a gap after the grantor gets possession.
Until Casey turns 25, Riley keeps the land.
When Casey turns 25, Casey’s interest springs out of Riley.

sections.title: 28. Rule Against Perpetuities
body: Certain future interests are valid only if they must vest or fail within:
A life in being plus 21 years
The law does not like future interests that might stay uncertain for too long.
“To Jordan for life, then to the first of Jordan’s descendants who becomes a doctor.”
RAP hates future interests that might stay uncertain too long.

blocks.text: Certain future interests are valid only if they must vest or fail within:
A life in being plus 21 years
The law does not like future interests that might stay uncertain for too long.
“To Jordan for life, then to the first of Jordan’s descendants who becomes a doctor.”
RAP hates future interests that might stay uncertain too long.

sections.title: 29. RAP Class Gift Rule
body: If a class gift violates RAP as to one class member, the whole class gift fails.
Bad as to one, bad as to all.

blocks.text: If a class gift violates RAP as to one class member, the whole class gift fails.
Bad as to one, bad as to all.

sections.title: 30. Rule of Convenience
body: A class closes when at least one class member is entitled to possession.
“To Jordan for life, then to Jordan’s nieces.”
When Jordan dies, the class of nieces closes.
Later-born nieces usually do not join.
Class closes when someone can take.

blocks.text: A class closes when at least one class member is entitled to possession.
“To Jordan for life, then to Jordan’s nieces.”
When Jordan dies, the class of nieces closes.
Later-born nieces usually do not join.
Class closes when someone can take.

sections.title: 31. Concurrent Estates
body: A concurrent estate exists when two or more people own property at the same time.

blocks.text: A concurrent estate exists when two or more people own property at the same time.

sections.title: 32. Tenancy in Common
body: Each cotenant has an equal right to possess the whole property.
There is no right of survivorship.
Maya and Leo own a duplex as tenants in common.
Maya’s share goes to Maya’s heirs or devisees, not automatically to Leo.

blocks.text: Each cotenant has an equal right to possess the whole property.
There is no right of survivorship.
Maya and Leo own a duplex as tenants in common.
Maya’s share goes to Maya’s heirs or devisees, not automatically to Leo.

sections.title: 33. Joint Tenancy
body: A joint tenancy includes a right of survivorship.
When one joint tenant dies, that person’s interest disappears, and the surviving joint tenants absorb it.
Maya and Leo own land as joint tenants with right of survivorship.
Maya’s will does not control Maya’s share.

blocks.text: A joint tenancy includes a right of survivorship.
When one joint tenant dies, that person’s interest disappears, and the surviving joint tenants absorb it.
Maya and Leo own land as joint tenants with right of survivorship.
Maya’s will does not control Maya’s share.

sections.title: 34. Severance of Joint Tenancy
body: A joint tenant can sever the joint tenancy by transferring their interest during life.
The result is usually a tenancy in common.
Maya and Leo are joint tenants.
Maya sells her share to Noah.
Noah and Leo become tenants in common.
A joint tenant cannot devise the interest by will because the interest disappears at death.
Lifetime transfer severs. Will does not.

blocks.text: A joint tenant can sever the joint tenancy by transferring their interest during life.
The result is usually a tenancy in common.
Maya and Leo are joint tenants.
Maya sells her share to Noah.
Noah and Leo become tenants in common.
A joint tenant cannot devise the interest by will because the interest disappears at death.
Lifetime transfer severs. Will does not.

sections.title: 35. Mortgage and Joint Tenancy
body: In most states, a mortgage is only a lien.
A mortgage does not sever joint tenancy until foreclosure.
In some states, a mortgage transfers title.
A mortgage can sever joint tenancy immediately.
Lien theory = no severance until foreclosure. Title theory = mortgage may sever.

blocks.text: In most states, a mortgage is only a lien.
A mortgage does not sever joint tenancy until foreclosure.
In some states, a mortgage transfers title.
A mortgage can sever joint tenancy immediately.
Lien theory = no severance until foreclosure. Title theory = mortgage may sever.

sections.title: 36. Tenancy by the Entirety
body: A tenancy by the entirety is like joint tenancy for married couples.
Neither spouse can transfer or mortgage the property alone.
Spouses Nina and Omar own a home as tenants by the entirety.
Nina cannot sell her half to someone else without Omar’s consent.
Tenancy by entirety = marriage + survivorship + both must agree.

blocks.text: A tenancy by the entirety is like joint tenancy for married couples.
Neither spouse can transfer or mortgage the property alone.
Spouses Nina and Omar own a home as tenants by the entirety.
Nina cannot sell her half to someone else without Omar’s consent.
Tenancy by entirety = marriage + survivorship + both must agree.

sections.title: 37. Cotenant Rights and Duties
body: Each cotenant has the right to possess the whole property.
One cotenant usually does not owe rent to the others merely because they live there.
If one cotenant blocks another from using the property, that is ouster.
A cotenant who pays necessary expenses may seek contribution.
A cotenant usually cannot force others to pay for improvements.
If one cotenant rents the property to a third party, the cotenant must share net rent with the other cotenants.
Cotenants share ownership, but one cotenant cannot force others to pay for upgrades.

blocks.text: Each cotenant has the right to possess the whole property.
One cotenant usually does not owe rent to the others merely because they live there.
If one cotenant blocks another from using the property, that is ouster.
A cotenant who pays necessary expenses may seek contribution.
A cotenant usually cannot force others to pay for improvements.
If one cotenant rents the property to a third party, the cotenant must share net rent with the other cotenants.
Cotenants share ownership, but one cotenant cannot force others to pay for upgrades.

sections.title: 38. Partition
body: A tenant in common or joint tenant can usually seek partition.
The court physically divides the property.
The court sells the property and divides the money.

blocks.text: A tenant in common or joint tenant can usually seek partition.
The court physically divides the property.
The court sells the property and divides the money.

sections.title: 39. Fair Housing Act
body: The Fair Housing Act prohibits discrimination in housing.
Sex includes sexual orientation and gender identity.
Familial status includes having children under 18 or being pregnant.
A person generally may not discriminate by:
A landlord refuses to rent to a family because they have a child.
That may violate the Fair Housing Act.
FHA = no protected-class discrimination in housing.

blocks.text: The Fair Housing Act prohibits discrimination in housing.
Sex includes sexual orientation and gender identity.
Familial status includes having children under 18 or being pregnant.
A person generally may not discriminate by:
A landlord refuses to rent to a family because they have a child.
That may violate the Fair Housing Act.
FHA = no protected-class discrimination in housing.

sections.title: 40. Conflict of Laws
body: For real property, the law of the place where the land is located usually controls.
Maya lives in State A but owns land in State B.
A dispute over ownership of that land is usually governed by State B law.

blocks.text: For real property, the law of the place where the land is located usually controls.
Maya lives in State A but owns land in State B.
A dispute over ownership of that land is usually governed by State B law.

sections.title: 41. Landlord-Tenant: Big Picture
body: Landlord-tenant questions usually ask: what tenancy, who breached, and what remedy?

blocks.text: Landlord-tenant questions usually ask: what tenancy, who breached, and what remedy?

sections.title: 42. Tenancy for Years
body: A tenancy for years lasts for a fixed period.
Maya leases an apartment to Leo from January 1 to December 31.
That is a tenancy for years.
It ends automatically at the end of the term.
Tenancy for years = fixed start and fixed end.

blocks.text: A tenancy for years lasts for a fixed period.
Maya leases an apartment to Leo from January 1 to December 31.
That is a tenancy for years.
It ends automatically at the end of the term.
Tenancy for years = fixed start and fixed end.

sections.title: 43. Periodic Tenancy
body: A periodic tenancy repeats automatically until proper notice is given.
Each month renews automatically unless one party gives proper notice.
Notice must usually be given before the last period begins.
For year-to-year tenancies, common law required six months’ notice.

blocks.text: A periodic tenancy repeats automatically until proper notice is given.
Each month renews automatically unless one party gives proper notice.
Notice must usually be given before the last period begins.
For year-to-year tenancies, common law required six months’ notice.

sections.title: 44. Tenancy at Will
body: A tenancy at will lasts as long as both landlord and tenant want.
Maya lets Leo stay in a guest apartment:
“You can stay as long as we both agree.”
That is a tenancy at will.
Tenancy at will = either side can end it.

blocks.text: A tenancy at will lasts as long as both landlord and tenant want.
Maya lets Leo stay in a guest apartment:
“You can stay as long as we both agree.”
That is a tenancy at will.
Tenancy at will = either side can end it.

sections.title: 45. Tenancy at Sufferance
body: A tenancy at sufferance happens when a tenant stays after the lease ends without permission.
This is also called a holdover tenancy.
Leo stays through July without permission.
Leo is a tenant at sufferance.
Bind Leo to a new periodic tenancy.

blocks.text: A tenancy at sufferance happens when a tenant stays after the lease ends without permission.
This is also called a holdover tenancy.
Leo stays through July without permission.
Leo is a tenant at sufferance.
Bind Leo to a new periodic tenancy.

sections.title: 46. Tenant Duties
body: A tenant must pay rent unless an exception applies.
Premises are destroyed without tenant’s fault
Warranty of habitability is breached, depending on state law
Tenant pays rent unless the law gives a reason not to.

blocks.text: A tenant must pay rent unless an exception applies.
Premises are destroyed without tenant’s fault
Warranty of habitability is breached, depending on state law
Tenant pays rent unless the law gives a reason not to.

sections.title: 47. Tenant Duty to Avoid Waste
body: Let the property deteriorate beyond normal wear and tear
Leo removes interior walls without permission.
Tenant must not ruin the property.

blocks.text: Let the property deteriorate beyond normal wear and tear
Leo removes interior walls without permission.
Tenant must not ruin the property.

sections.title: 48. Tenant Abandonment
body: If a tenant unjustifiably leaves before the lease ends, the tenant is treated as offering surrender.
Accept surrender and end the lease.
Refuse surrender and hold tenant liable.
Re-rent and hold tenant liable for deficiency.
Most states require the landlord to make reasonable efforts to re-rent.
Leo leaves his apartment six months early.
Maya must usually try to find a new tenant.
Tenant abandons = landlord should usually mitigate.

blocks.text: If a tenant unjustifiably leaves before the lease ends, the tenant is treated as offering surrender.
Accept surrender and end the lease.
Refuse surrender and hold tenant liable.
Re-rent and hold tenant liable for deficiency.
Most states require the landlord to make reasonable efforts to re-rent.
Leo leaves his apartment six months early.
Maya must usually try to find a new tenant.
Tenant abandons = landlord should usually mitigate.

sections.title: 49. Holdover Tenant
body: If a tenant stays after the lease ends, the landlord may:
Bind the tenant to a new periodic tenancy.
Leo stays and Maya accepts rent.
A new periodic tenancy may be created.

blocks.text: If a tenant stays after the lease ends, the landlord may:
Bind the tenant to a new periodic tenancy.
Leo stays and Maya accepts rent.
A new periodic tenancy may be created.

sections.title: 50. Landlord Duty to Deliver Possession
body: The English Rule is the majority rule.
The landlord must deliver actual possession.
The American Rule is the minority rule.
The landlord only must deliver the legal right to possess.
Maya leases a store to Leo.
The old tenant refuses to leave.
Under the English Rule, Maya has failed to deliver possession.
Under the American Rule, Leo must deal with the holdover.
English rule = landlord must get you in. American rule = landlord gives right, tenant fights.

blocks.text: The English Rule is the majority rule.
The landlord must deliver actual possession.
The American Rule is the minority rule.
The landlord only must deliver the legal right to possess.
Maya leases a store to Leo.
The old tenant refuses to leave.
Under the English Rule, Maya has failed to deliver possession.
Under the American Rule, Leo must deal with the holdover.
English rule = landlord must get you in. American rule = landlord gives right, tenant fights.

sections.title: 51. Implied Warranty of Habitability
body: Residential leases include an implied warranty that the premises are fit for human living.
Conditions that threaten health or safety.
Treat the breach as constructive eviction.
Usually, the tenant must notify the landlord and give reasonable time to repair.
Habitability = residential property must be livable.

blocks.text: Residential leases include an implied warranty that the premises are fit for human living.
Conditions that threaten health or safety.
Treat the breach as constructive eviction.
Usually, the tenant must notify the landlord and give reasonable time to repair.
Habitability = residential property must be livable.

sections.title: 52. Covenant of Quiet Enjoyment
body: Every lease includes a promise that the landlord will not interfere with the tenant’s possession.
Quiet enjoyment = tenant’s right to possess without landlord interference.

blocks.text: Every lease includes a promise that the landlord will not interfere with the tenant’s possession.
Quiet enjoyment = tenant’s right to possess without landlord interference.

sections.title: 53. Actual Eviction
body: Actual eviction occurs when the landlord physically or legally excludes the tenant from the premises.
The tenant usually owes no rent for the entire premises.
Maya leases a whole office suite to Leo but locks Leo out of one room.
That may be partial actual eviction by the landlord.
Landlord partial eviction can excuse all rent.

blocks.text: Actual eviction occurs when the landlord physically or legally excludes the tenant from the premises.
The tenant usually owes no rent for the entire premises.
Maya leases a whole office suite to Leo but locks Leo out of one room.
That may be partial actual eviction by the landlord.
Landlord partial eviction can excuse all rent.

sections.title: 54. Constructive Eviction
body: Constructive eviction occurs when the landlord’s breach substantially interferes with the tenant’s use and enjoyment.
Landlord had reasonable time to fix.
The heating system fails in winter.
Maya refuses to repair after notice.
Constructive eviction = bad condition + notice + no fix + tenant leaves.

blocks.text: Constructive eviction occurs when the landlord’s breach substantially interferes with the tenant’s use and enjoyment.
Landlord had reasonable time to fix.
The heating system fails in winter.
Maya refuses to repair after notice.
Constructive eviction = bad condition + notice + no fix + tenant leaves.

sections.title: 55. Retaliatory Eviction
body: A landlord cannot evict a residential tenant for good-faith complaints about housing violations.
Leo reports dangerous wiring to the city.
Maya tries to evict Leo because of the complaint.
Landlord cannot punish tenant for asserting housing rights.

blocks.text: A landlord cannot evict a residential tenant for good-faith complaints about housing violations.
Leo reports dangerous wiring to the city.
Maya tries to evict Leo because of the complaint.
Landlord cannot punish tenant for asserting housing rights.

sections.title: 56. Assignment and Sublease
body: An assignment happens when a tenant transfers the entire remaining lease term.
A sublease happens when a tenant transfers less than the entire remaining lease term.
Leo transfers all 10 months to Nora.
Assignment = whole remaining term. Sublease = less than whole term.

blocks.text: An assignment happens when a tenant transfers the entire remaining lease term.
A sublease happens when a tenant transfers less than the entire remaining lease term.
Leo transfers all 10 months to Nora.
Assignment = whole remaining term. Sublease = less than whole term.

sections.title: 57. Assignee Liability
body: An assignee is in privity of estate with the landlord.
The assignee is liable for rent and covenants that run with the lease while the assignee holds the lease.
The original tenant remains liable because of privity of contract, unless released.
Assignment removes tenant from estate, not from contract.

blocks.text: An assignee is in privity of estate with the landlord.
The assignee is liable for rent and covenants that run with the lease while the assignee holds the lease.
The original tenant remains liable because of privity of contract, unless released.
Assignment removes tenant from estate, not from contract.

sections.title: 58. Sublessee Liability
body: A sublessee is usually not in privity with the landlord.
The sublessee pays the original tenant, not the landlord.
If the sublessee expressly assumes lease duties, the sublessee may be personally liable.
Sublease keeps landlord and subtenant apart.

blocks.text: A sublessee is usually not in privity with the landlord.
The sublessee pays the original tenant, not the landlord.
If the sublessee expressly assumes lease duties, the sublessee may be personally liable.
Sublease keeps landlord and subtenant apart.

sections.title: 59. Condemnation
body: Condemnation is government taking land for public use or because it is unfit.
Tenant usually continues paying rent but may receive compensation for the part taken.
Complete taking ends rent. Partial taking usually does not.

blocks.text: Condemnation is government taking land for public use or because it is unfit.
Tenant usually continues paying rent but may receive compensation for the part taken.
Complete taking ends rent. Partial taking usually does not.

sections.title: 60. Land-Sales Contract
body: Signed by the party to be charged
Land sale = contract rules + Statute of Frauds.

blocks.text: Signed by the party to be charged
Land sale = contract rules + Statute of Frauds.

sections.title: 61. Broker Rules
body: A broker who is an agent owes fiduciary duties, including:
A broker usually earns commission by producing a ready, willing, and able buyer on the seller’s terms.
Broker gets paid when broker produces the deal required by the listing.

blocks.text: A broker who is an agent owes fiduciary duties, including:
A broker usually earns commission by producing a ready, willing, and able buyer on the seller’s terms.
Broker gets paid when broker produces the deal required by the listing.

sections.title: 62. Statute of Frauds Exceptions
body: An oral land contract may be enforced if acts strongly show a contract existed.
Most states require at least two.
If one side fully performs, the oral promise may be enforced.
If one party reasonably relied and would suffer hardship, the other may be estopped from using the Statute of Frauds.
If the other party admits the contract, enforcement may be allowed.
Part performance can rescue an oral land deal.

blocks.text: An oral land contract may be enforced if acts strongly show a contract existed.
Most states require at least two.
If one side fully performs, the oral promise may be enforced.
If one party reasonably relied and would suffer hardship, the other may be estopped from using the Statute of Frauds.
If the other party admits the contract, enforcement may be allowed.
Part performance can rescue an oral land deal.

sections.title: 63. Marketable Title
body: Seller must provide marketable title unless the contract says otherwise.
Marketable title is title free from defects that create unreasonable risk of litigation.
Title may be unmarketable because of:
Seller usually must deliver marketable title at closing, not before.
Marketable title = buyer should not be buying a lawsuit.

blocks.text: Seller must provide marketable title unless the contract says otherwise.
Marketable title is title free from defects that create unreasonable risk of litigation.
Title may be unmarketable because of:
Seller usually must deliver marketable title at closing, not before.
Marketable title = buyer should not be buying a lawsuit.

sections.title: 64. Time of the Essence
body: Time is not automatically of the essence in land-sale contracts.
Time Is of the Essence If
If Time Is Not of the Essence
Late performance may still be okay if within a reasonable time.
Real estate closing dates are flexible unless time is clearly essential.

blocks.text: Time is not automatically of the essence in land-sale contracts.
Time Is of the Essence If
If Time Is Not of the Essence
Late performance may still be okay if within a reasonable time.
Real estate closing dates are flexible unless time is clearly essential.

sections.title: 65. Implied Warranty for New Homes
body: Most states imply a warranty that a new home was built with proper workmanship and is suitable for living.
Usually commercial builders, developers, and contractors.
A newly built house has hidden faulty wiring that appears after sale.
New home from builder = implied promise it was built properly.

blocks.text: Most states imply a warranty that a new home was built with proper workmanship and is suitable for living.
Usually commercial builders, developers, and contractors.
A newly built house has hidden faulty wiring that appears after sale.
New home from builder = implied promise it was built properly.

sections.title: 66. Duty to Disclose Defects
body: Most states require residential sellers to disclose known material physical defects that are not obvious.
Seller knows the basement floods every spring but says nothing.
Buyer may rescind or sue for damages.
Known hidden material defect must usually be disclosed.

blocks.text: Most states require residential sellers to disclose known material physical defects that are not obvious.
Seller knows the basement floods every spring but says nothing.
Buyer may rescind or sue for damages.
Known hidden material defect must usually be disclosed.

sections.title: 67. Merger
body: At closing, the land-sale contract merges into the deed.
After closing, buyer usually cannot sue on contract promises unless they are also in the deed or survive merger.
The contract says seller will include a storage shed.
After closing, buyer may have trouble enforcing that promise unless it survives merger.
After closing, look to the deed.

blocks.text: At closing, the land-sale contract merges into the deed.
After closing, buyer usually cannot sue on contract promises unless they are also in the deed or survive merger.
The contract says seller will include a storage shed.
After closing, buyer may have trouble enforcing that promise unless it survives merger.
After closing, look to the deed.

sections.title: 68. Land-Sale Remedies
body: Buyer or seller may recover expectation damages:
Difference between contract price and market value at performance
Because land is unique, the buyer usually can force the seller to convey.
The seller may also seek specific performance in many courts.
Buyer may seek specific performance with a price reduction for a defect.
Land is unique, so specific performance is common.

blocks.text: Buyer or seller may recover expectation damages:
Difference between contract price and market value at performance
Because land is unique, the buyer usually can force the seller to convey.
The seller may also seek specific performance in many courts.
Buyer may seek specific performance with a price reduction for a defect.
Land is unique, so specific performance is common.

sections.title: 69. Equitable Conversion
body: Once a specifically enforceable land-sale contract is signed:
Seller keeps legal title until closing.
Seller’s interest becomes the right to receive money.
Maya contracts to sell land to Leo.
Before closing, Maya’s creditor gets a judgment against Maya.
Because Leo already has equitable title, the judgment usually does not attach to the land.
Most states put risk of loss on the buyer after contract.
The minority rule keeps risk with the seller until the buyer gets possession or title.
After contract, buyer owns land in equity; seller owns the money claim.

blocks.text: Once a specifically enforceable land-sale contract is signed:
Seller keeps legal title until closing.
Seller’s interest becomes the right to receive money.
Maya contracts to sell land to Leo.
Before closing, Maya’s creditor gets a judgment against Maya.
Because Leo already has equitable title, the judgment usually does not attach to the land.
Most states put risk of loss on the buyer after contract.
The minority rule keeps risk with the seller until the buyer gets possession or title.
After contract, buyer owns land in equity; seller owns the money claim.

sections.title: 70. Options and Rights of First Refusal
body: An option gives someone the right to buy property within a set time.
The offer cannot be revoked during the option period.
Maya pays Leo $1,000 for the option to buy Leo’s building within 60 days.
Leo cannot revoke during those 60 days.
A right of first refusal gives the holder the first chance to buy if the owner decides to sell.
Maya has a right of first refusal.
If Leo decides to sell, Leo must give Maya the chance to buy before selling to someone else.
Option = right to buy. ROFR = first chance if owner sells.

blocks.text: An option gives someone the right to buy property within a set time.
The offer cannot be revoked during the option period.
Maya pays Leo $1,000 for the option to buy Leo’s building within 60 days.
Leo cannot revoke during those 60 days.
A right of first refusal gives the holder the first chance to buy if the owner decides to sell.
Maya has a right of first refusal.
If Leo decides to sell, Leo must give Maya the chance to buy before selling to someone else.
Option = right to buy. ROFR = first chance if owner sells.

sections.title: 71. Adverse Possession
body: Adverse possession transfers ownership to someone who possesses land long enough in the required way.
The possessor cannot share possession with the true owner.
Use must continue for the statutory period.
Seasonal use can count if appropriate for the property.
Possession is without the owner’s permission.
Possession must be visible enough that a reasonable owner would notice.
Noah fences part of vacant land, plants a garden there every year, excludes others, and treats it as his own for the statutory period.
Noah may acquire title by adverse possession.
Adverse possession = possess like an owner long enough.

blocks.text: Adverse possession transfers ownership to someone who possesses land long enough in the required way.
The possessor cannot share possession with the true owner.
Use must continue for the statutory period.
Seasonal use can count if appropriate for the property.
Possession is without the owner’s permission.
Possession must be visible enough that a reasonable owner would notice.
Noah fences part of vacant land, plants a garden there every year, excludes others, and treats it as his own for the statutory period.
Noah may acquire title by adverse possession.
Adverse possession = possess like an owner long enough.

sections.title: 72. Tacking
body: An adverse possessor can add a predecessor’s time if there is privity.
A voluntary transfer connects the possessors.
Noah sells his interest to Maya.
If the period is 10 years, Maya may tack Noah’s time.

blocks.text: An adverse possessor can add a predecessor’s time if there is privity.
A voluntary transfer connects the possessors.
Noah sells his interest to Maya.
If the period is 10 years, Maya may tack Noah’s time.

sections.title: 73. Disability
body: If the true owner has a disability when adverse possession begins, the statute may be tolled.
Disability must exist at the start of adverse possession.
A later disability does not stop the clock.
Disability matters only if it exists at the beginning.

blocks.text: If the true owner has a disability when adverse possession begins, the statute may be tolled.
Disability must exist at the start of adverse possession.
A later disability does not stop the clock.
Disability matters only if it exists at the beginning.

sections.title: 74. Deeds
body: A deed transfers a real property interest.
That can be a valid deed if the description is sufficient.
Deed needs who, what, transfer words, and grantor signature.

blocks.text: A deed transfers a real property interest.
That can be a valid deed if the description is sufficient.
Deed needs who, what, transfer words, and grantor signature.

sections.title: 75. Delivery of Deed
body: Delivery means the grantor intended to make a present transfer.
Physical handoff is evidence but not required.
If the grantor hands the deed to the grantee with an oral condition not in the deed, the oral condition is usually ignored.
Maya gives Leo a deed and says:
“This only counts if you get married next year.”
If the deed itself has no condition, the oral condition may be ignored.

blocks.text: Delivery means the grantor intended to make a present transfer.
Physical handoff is evidence but not required.
If the grantor hands the deed to the grantee with an oral condition not in the deed, the oral condition is usually ignored.
Maya gives Leo a deed and says:
“This only counts if you get married next year.”
If the deed itself has no condition, the oral condition may be ignored.

sections.title: 76. Acceptance
body: The grantee must accept the deed.
Acceptance is presumed if the transfer benefits the grantee.
Once the grantee accepts, giving the deed back does not undo the transfer.
Accepted deed transfers title; return of paper does not transfer it back.

blocks.text: The grantee must accept the deed.
Acceptance is presumed if the transfer benefits the grantee.
Once the grantee accepts, giving the deed back does not undo the transfer.
Accepted deed transfers title; return of paper does not transfer it back.

sections.title: 77. Forgery and Fraud
body: No one can get good title through a forged deed, even a bona fide purchaser.
Fraud in the factum is void.
The grantor did not know they were signing a deed.
Fraud in the inducement is voidable.
A bona fide purchaser may be protected.
Maya tricks Leo into signing a deed by saying it is a birthday card.
That is fraud in the factum.
Forgery and fraud in factum = void. Fraud in inducement = voidable.

blocks.text: No one can get good title through a forged deed, even a bona fide purchaser.
Fraud in the factum is void.
The grantor did not know they were signing a deed.
Fraud in the inducement is voidable.
A bona fide purchaser may be protected.
Maya tricks Leo into signing a deed by saying it is a birthday card.
That is fraud in the factum.
Forgery and fraud in factum = void. Fraud in inducement = voidable.

sections.title: 78. Recording Acts
body: A deed is valid between the parties even if not recorded.
Recording protects against later competing claimants.
Recording acts decide who wins when there are multiple claimants.

blocks.text: A deed is valid between the parties even if not recorded.
Recording protects against later competing claimants.
Recording acts decide who wins when there are multiple claimants.

sections.title: 79. Notice Statute
body: A later purchaser wins if they paid value and had no notice of the earlier interest.
They do not have to record first to beat the earlier claimant.
Maya sells land to Leo, who does not record.
Maya later sells to Nora, who pays value and has no notice.
In a notice state, Nora wins.
Notice statute = later BFP without notice wins.

blocks.text: A later purchaser wins if they paid value and had no notice of the earlier interest.
They do not have to record first to beat the earlier claimant.
Maya sells land to Leo, who does not record.
Maya later sells to Nora, who pays value and has no notice.
In a notice state, Nora wins.
Notice statute = later BFP without notice wins.

sections.title: 80. Race Statute
body: Whoever records first wins, even if they had notice.
Nora knows about Leo but records first.
In a race state, Nora wins.
Race statute = first to record wins.

blocks.text: Whoever records first wins, even if they had notice.
Nora knows about Leo but records first.
In a race state, Nora wins.
Race statute = first to record wins.

sections.title: 81. Race-Notice Statute
body: A later purchaser wins only if they:
Maya sells to Leo, who does not record.
Maya later sells to Nora, who has no notice.
If Nora records before Leo, Nora wins.
If Leo records first, Leo wins.

blocks.text: A later purchaser wins only if they:
Maya sells to Leo, who does not record.
Maya later sells to Nora, who has no notice.
If Nora records before Leo, Nora wins.
If Leo records first, Leo wins.

sections.title: 82. Types of Notice
body: A reasonable inspection would reveal the prior interest.
The prior interest is properly recorded in the chain of title.
Nora buys a house from Maya, but Leo is living there.
Nora must ask why Leo is there.

blocks.text: A reasonable inspection would reveal the prior interest.
The prior interest is properly recorded in the chain of title.
Nora buys a house from Maya, but Leo is living there.
Nora must ask why Leo is there.

sections.title: 83. Wild Deed
body: A wild deed is recorded but outside the chain of title.
It does not give constructive notice.
Maya sells to Leo, but Leo does not record.
Leo sells to Nora, and Nora records.
Because Leo’s deed from Maya was never recorded, Nora’s deed is floating outside the chain.
Wild deed = recorded but hidden because a prior link is missing.

blocks.text: A wild deed is recorded but outside the chain of title.
It does not give constructive notice.
Maya sells to Leo, but Leo does not record.
Leo sells to Nora, and Nora records.
Because Leo’s deed from Maya was never recorded, Nora’s deed is floating outside the chain.
Wild deed = recorded but hidden because a prior link is missing.

sections.title: 84. Shelter Rule
body: A person who takes from a protected purchaser gets that purchaser’s protection.
Nora is a bona fide purchaser protected by the recording act.
Nora sells to Owen, who knows about a prior claim.
Owen may still be protected under the shelter rule.
A person with notice cannot use a straw transaction to clean title and buy it back.
Shelter rule = protected title shelters later grantees.

blocks.text: A person who takes from a protected purchaser gets that purchaser’s protection.
Nora is a bona fide purchaser protected by the recording act.
Nora sells to Owen, who knows about a prior claim.
Owen may still be protected under the shelter rule.
A person with notice cannot use a straw transaction to clean title and buy it back.
Shelter rule = protected title shelters later grantees.

sections.title: 85. Title Insurance
body: Title insurance protects against covered title defects.
Title insurance pays for covered title problems.

blocks.text: Title insurance protects against covered title defects.
Title insurance pays for covered title problems.

sections.title: 86. Types of Deeds
body: A general warranty deed gives the strongest promises.
A special warranty deed only promises against defects arising during the grantor’s ownership.
A quitclaim deed makes no title promises.
General warranty = big promises. Special warranty = my-time promises. Quitclaim = no promises.

blocks.text: A general warranty deed gives the strongest promises.
A special warranty deed only promises against defects arising during the grantor’s ownership.
A quitclaim deed makes no title promises.
General warranty = big promises. Special warranty = my-time promises. Quitclaim = no promises.

sections.title: 87. General Warranty Deed Covenants
body: Breach occurs when possession is disturbed.
Present covenants breached now. Future covenants breached later.

blocks.text: Breach occurs when possession is disturbed.
Present covenants breached now. Future covenants breached later.

sections.title: 88. After-Acquired Title
body: If a grantor conveys property they do not own but later acquires it, title automatically passes to the earlier grantee under warranty deed principles.
Maya deeds land to Leo before she owns it.
Later Maya actually acquires the land.
Grantor later gets it, earlier grantee gets benefit.

blocks.text: If a grantor conveys property they do not own but later acquires it, title automatically passes to the earlier grantee under warranty deed principles.
Maya deeds land to Leo before she owns it.
Later Maya actually acquires the land.
Grantor later gets it, earlier grantee gets benefit.

sections.title: 89. Conveyance by Will
body: Real property can pass at death by:
A specific devise is a gift of specific property.
A residuary clause is a gift of everything not specifically given.
“My house to Leo. Everything else to Nora.”
The house is a specific devise.

blocks.text: Real property can pass at death by:
A specific devise is a gift of specific property.
A residuary clause is a gift of everything not specifically given.
“My house to Leo. Everything else to Nora.”
The house is a specific devise.

sections.title: 90. Exoneration of Liens
body: If specific real property is devised with a mortgage, the estate pays off the mortgage from remaining assets.
The devisee takes subject to the mortgage unless the will says otherwise.
A will gives a house to Leo.
In modern states, Leo usually takes the house with the mortgage unless the will says the estate must pay it.
Common law estate pays mortgage. Modern law devisee usually takes subject to mortgage.

blocks.text: If specific real property is devised with a mortgage, the estate pays off the mortgage from remaining assets.
The devisee takes subject to the mortgage unless the will says otherwise.
A will gives a house to Leo.
In modern states, Leo usually takes the house with the mortgage unless the will says the estate must pay it.
Common law estate pays mortgage. Modern law devisee usually takes subject to mortgage.

sections.title: 91. Trusts
body: A trust lets a trustee manage property for beneficiaries.
A charitable trust must have a charitable purpose and benefit a broad group.
Trust = trustee holds legal title for beneficiary.

blocks.text: A trust lets a trustee manage property for beneficiaries.
A charitable trust must have a charitable purpose and benefit a broad group.
Trust = trustee holds legal title for beneficiary.

sections.title: 92. Restraints on Alienation
body: A restraint on alienation limits transfer of property.
Direct restraints on fee simple are disfavored and often invalid.
Law dislikes total bans on selling land.

blocks.text: A restraint on alienation limits transfer of property.
Direct restraints on fee simple are disfavored and often invalid.
Law dislikes total bans on selling land.

sections.title: 93. Mortgages
body: A mortgage is a security interest in real property for a debt.
Leo gives Bank a mortgage on his house.
Mortgagor gives mortgage. Mortgagee gets mortgage.

blocks.text: A mortgage is a security interest in real property for a debt.
Leo gives Bank a mortgage on his house.
Mortgagor gives mortgage. Mortgagee gets mortgage.

sections.title: 94. Lien Theory vs. Title Theory
body: Lender technically holds title until the debt is paid.
Lien theory: mortgage does not sever joint tenancy until foreclosure.
Title theory: mortgage may sever joint tenancy immediately.
Lien theory = borrower owns. Title theory = lender has title.

blocks.text: Lender technically holds title until the debt is paid.
Lien theory: mortgage does not sever joint tenancy until foreclosure.
Title theory: mortgage may sever joint tenancy immediately.
Lien theory = borrower owns. Title theory = lender has title.

sections.title: 95. Deed of Trust
body: A deed of trust works like a mortgage but involves a third-party trustee.
Leo gives title to Trustee as security for Bank’s loan.
If Leo defaults, Trustee can sell the property.
Deed of trust = mortgage with trustee.

blocks.text: A deed of trust works like a mortgage but involves a third-party trustee.
Leo gives title to Trustee as security for Bank’s loan.
If Leo defaults, Trustee can sell the property.
Deed of trust = mortgage with trustee.

sections.title: 96. Installment Land Contract
body: An installment land contract means the buyer pays in installments while the seller keeps title until final payment.
Many states protect defaulting buyers by:
Leo buys land from Maya by paying monthly for 10 years.
Maya keeps title until the last payment.
That is an installment land contract.
Installment land contract = pay over time, seller keeps title.

blocks.text: An installment land contract means the buyer pays in installments while the seller keeps title until final payment.
Many states protect defaulting buyers by:
Leo buys land from Maya by paying monthly for 10 years.
Maya keeps title until the last payment.
That is an installment land contract.
Installment land contract = pay over time, seller keeps title.

sections.title: 97. Absolute Deed as Disguised Mortgage
body: A deed that looks like an outright sale may actually be a mortgage if it was intended as security for a debt.
Leo deeds land to Maya, but they agree Maya will return it if Leo repays.
A court may treat this as a mortgage.
If deed is really security, court may call it a mortgage.

blocks.text: A deed that looks like an outright sale may actually be a mortgage if it was intended as security for a debt.
Leo deeds land to Maya, but they agree Maya will return it if Leo repays.
A court may treat this as a mortgage.
If deed is really security, court may call it a mortgage.

sections.title: 98. Transfer of Mortgaged Property
body: The original borrower remains personally liable unless the lender releases them.
The buyer agrees to pay the mortgage.
The original borrower remains secondarily liable unless released.
The buyer takes property subject to the mortgage but does not personally agree to pay.
If default occurs, the lender can foreclose the property but cannot sue the buyer personally for deficiency.
Assume = personal liability. Subject to = property at risk, buyer not personally liable.

blocks.text: The original borrower remains personally liable unless the lender releases them.
The buyer agrees to pay the mortgage.
The original borrower remains secondarily liable unless released.
The buyer takes property subject to the mortgage but does not personally agree to pay.
If default occurs, the lender can foreclose the property but cannot sue the buyer personally for deficiency.
Assume = personal liability. Subject to = property at risk, buyer not personally liable.

sections.title: 99. Due-on-Sale Clause
body: A due-on-sale clause lets the lender demand full payment if property is transferred without permission.
The lender generally cannot enforce a due-on-sale clause for certain transfers, including:
Due-on-sale is broad, but protected residential transfers are exceptions.

blocks.text: A due-on-sale clause lets the lender demand full payment if property is transferred without permission.
The lender generally cannot enforce a due-on-sale clause for certain transfers, including:
Due-on-sale is broad, but protected residential transfers are exceptions.

sections.title: 100. Transfer of Mortgage and Note
body: The note and mortgage usually travel together.
The mortgage usually follows the note automatically.
Some say mortgage transfer alone is void.
The note is the debt; the mortgage is the security.

blocks.text: The note and mortgage usually travel together.
The mortgage usually follows the note automatically.
Some say mortgage transfer alone is void.
The note is the debt; the mortgage is the security.

sections.title: 101. Equity of Redemption
body: After default but before foreclosure sale, the borrower can redeem by paying what is owed.
If an acceleration clause applies, the borrower may need to pay the full loan balance.
The lender cannot force the borrower to waive the redemption right in the mortgage itself.
Before foreclosure sale, borrower can redeem.

blocks.text: After default but before foreclosure sale, the borrower can redeem by paying what is owed.
If an acceleration clause applies, the borrower may need to pay the full loan balance.
The lender cannot force the borrower to waive the redemption right in the mortgage itself.
Before foreclosure sale, borrower can redeem.

sections.title: 102. Statutory Redemption
body: Some states allow the borrower to redeem after foreclosure sale.
After foreclosure, Leo may have six months to pay the foreclosure purchaser and reclaim the property if the state gives statutory redemption.
Equity redemption = before sale. Statutory redemption = after sale.

blocks.text: Some states allow the borrower to redeem after foreclosure sale.
After foreclosure, Leo may have six months to pay the foreclosure purchaser and reclaim the property if the state gives statutory redemption.
Equity redemption = before sale. Statutory redemption = after sale.

sections.title: 103. Foreclosure
body: A lender can foreclose after material default.
An acceleration clause lets the lender declare the full debt due upon default.
Foreclosure sells land to satisfy debt.

blocks.text: A lender can foreclose after material default.
An acceleration clause lets the lender declare the full debt due upon default.
Foreclosure sells land to satisfy debt.

sections.title: 104. Foreclosure Priority
body: Foreclosure destroys junior interests but not senior interests.
A junior interest is usually wiped out if properly joined or noticed.
First Bank has the first mortgage.
Second Bank has the second mortgage.
Foreclosure wipes out below, not above.

blocks.text: Foreclosure destroys junior interests but not senior interests.
A junior interest is usually wiped out if properly joined or noticed.
First Bank has the first mortgage.
Second Bank has the second mortgage.
Foreclosure wipes out below, not above.

sections.title: 105. Purchase-Money Mortgage
body: A purchase-money mortgage helps the buyer acquire the property.
Purchase-money mortgages have special priority over earlier claims against the buyer.
Leo has an old judgment lien against him.
Leo buys a house using Bank’s loan secured by a purchase-money mortgage.
Bank’s purchase-money mortgage usually beats the old judgment lien.
Purchase-money mortgage jumps ahead of buyer’s old liens.

blocks.text: A purchase-money mortgage helps the buyer acquire the property.
Purchase-money mortgages have special priority over earlier claims against the buyer.
Leo has an old judgment lien against him.
Leo buys a house using Bank’s loan secured by a purchase-money mortgage.
Bank’s purchase-money mortgage usually beats the old judgment lien.
Purchase-money mortgage jumps ahead of buyer’s old liens.

sections.title: 107. Deficiency
body: If a foreclosure sale does not cover the debt, the lender may seek a deficiency judgment against liable parties.
Deficiency = lender sues for unpaid balance after foreclosure.

blocks.text: If a foreclosure sale does not cover the debt, the lender may seek a deficiency judgment against liable parties.
Deficiency = lender sues for unpaid balance after foreclosure.

sections.title: 108. Subrogation
body: A person who pays another’s mortgage may step into the lender’s shoes to avoid unjust enrichment.
Nora pays off Leo’s mortgage to protect her own junior lien.
Nora may be subrogated to the senior lender’s priority.
Subrogation = pay debt, step into creditor’s shoes.

blocks.text: A person who pays another’s mortgage may step into the lender’s shoes to avoid unjust enrichment.
Nora pays off Leo’s mortgage to protect her own junior lien.
Nora may be subrogated to the senior lender’s priority.
Subrogation = pay debt, step into creditor’s shoes.

sections.title: 109. Easements
body: An easement is a limited right to use someone else’s land.
Leo has the right to cross Maya’s driveway to reach his garage.
Easement = right to use someone else’s land.

blocks.text: An easement is a limited right to use someone else’s land.
Leo has the right to cross Maya’s driveway to reach his garage.
Easement = right to use someone else’s land.

sections.title: 110. Easement Types
body: Right to do something on land.
Right to stop the landowner from doing something.
Appurtenant benefits land. In gross benefits person.

blocks.text: Right to do something on land.
Right to stop the landowner from doing something.
Appurtenant benefits land. In gross benefits person.

sections.title: 112. Express Easement
body: An express easement is created in writing satisfying deed requirements.
Maya signs a deed giving Leo the right to use a path across Maya’s land.
Express easement = written permission with property effect.

blocks.text: An express easement is created in writing satisfying deed requirements.
Maya signs a deed giving Leo the right to use a path across Maya’s land.
Express easement = written permission with property effect.

sections.title: 113. Easement by Necessity
body: An easement by necessity is created when land is virtually useless without access.
Maya owns a large parcel and sells the back portion to Leo.
Leo’s portion has no access to any public street except across Maya’s retained land.
Leo may have an easement by necessity.

blocks.text: An easement by necessity is created when land is virtually useless without access.
Maya owns a large parcel and sells the back portion to Leo.
Leo’s portion has no access to any public street except across Maya’s retained land.
Leo may have an easement by necessity.

sections.title: 114. Easement by Implication
body: An easement by implication is created from prior use when land was once commonly owned.
Maya owns two lots and uses a drain across one lot to benefit the other.
A court may imply continued use of the drain.
Implication = prior use shows parties intended it to continue.

blocks.text: An easement by implication is created from prior use when land was once commonly owned.
Maya owns two lots and uses a drain across one lot to benefit the other.
A court may imply continued use of the drain.
Implication = prior use shows parties intended it to continue.

sections.title: 115. Easement by Prescription
body: An easement by prescription is similar to adverse possession, but for use rather than ownership.
Leo openly uses a footpath across Maya’s land without permission for the statutory period.
Leo may get a prescriptive easement.

blocks.text: An easement by prescription is similar to adverse possession, but for use rather than ownership.
Leo openly uses a footpath across Maya’s land without permission for the statutory period.
Leo may get a prescriptive easement.

sections.title: 116. Easement by Estoppel
body: Permission plus reasonable detrimental reliance can create an easement.
Maya tells Leo he may build a walkway across Maya’s land to reach his studio.
Maya may be estopped from revoking permission.
Estoppel = permission + reliance + injustice if revoked.

blocks.text: Permission plus reasonable detrimental reliance can create an easement.
Maya tells Leo he may build a walkway across Maya’s land to reach his studio.
Maya may be estopped from revoking permission.
Estoppel = permission + reliance + injustice if revoked.

sections.title: 117. Easement Scope
body: Scope is determined by the writing.
Scope is determined by prior use.
Scope is determined by adverse use.
Easement scope depends on how it was created.

blocks.text: Scope is determined by the writing.
Scope is determined by prior use.
Scope is determined by adverse use.
Easement scope depends on how it was created.

sections.title: 118. Easement Termination
body: Easements can end by release, merger, abandonment, necessity ending, or priority problems.

blocks.text: Easements can end by release, merger, abandonment, necessity ending, or priority problems.

sections.title: 119. License
body: A license is permission to use land.
Maya lets Leo enter her backyard for one afternoon to take photos.
A license usually does not create a permanent property right.
A license may become irrevocable if it is:

blocks.text: A license is permission to use land.
Maya lets Leo enter her backyard for one afternoon to take photos.
A license usually does not create a permanent property right.
A license may become irrevocable if it is:

sections.title: 120. Profit
body: A profit is a right to enter land and remove natural resources.
Maya gives Leo the right to enter her land and remove stone.
Profit = enter and take natural resources.

blocks.text: A profit is a right to enter land and remove natural resources.
Maya gives Leo the right to enter her land and remove stone.
Profit = enter and take natural resources.

sections.title: 121. Real Covenants
body: A real covenant is a promise about land use enforceable by money damages.
Notice for burden to bind purchaser
Two neighbors agree in a recorded deed that each owner must maintain a shared fence.
If a later owner refuses, damages may be available if the covenant runs.
Real covenant = land promise enforced by damages.

blocks.text: A real covenant is a promise about land use enforceable by money damages.
Notice for burden to bind purchaser
Two neighbors agree in a recorded deed that each owner must maintain a shared fence.
If a later owner refuses, damages may be available if the covenant runs.
Real covenant = land promise enforced by damages.

sections.title: 122. Equitable Servitudes
body: An equitable servitude is a land-use promise enforced by injunction.
A subdivision restriction says homes may only be used for residential purposes.
A neighbor seeks an injunction against business use.
Equitable servitude = land promise enforced by injunction.

blocks.text: An equitable servitude is a land-use promise enforced by injunction.
A subdivision restriction says homes may only be used for residential purposes.
A neighbor seeks an injunction against business use.
Equitable servitude = land promise enforced by injunction.

sections.title: 123. Real Covenant vs. Equitable Servitude
body: Notice? — Needed for burden against purchaser — Needed against purchaser

blocks.text: Notice? — Needed for burden against purchaser — Needed against purchaser

sections.title: 124. Implied Reciprocal Servitude
body: A restriction may be implied in a subdivision if there is a common scheme and notice.
Almost every deed says no commercial buildings.
Buyer of one lot without that language may still be bound if buyer had notice of the common plan.

blocks.text: A restriction may be implied in a subdivision if there is a common scheme and notice.
Almost every deed says no commercial buildings.
Buyer of one lot without that language may still be bound if buyer had notice of the common plan.

sections.title: 125. Defenses to Equitable Servitudes
body: A neighborhood restriction says only single-family homes.
Decades later, the entire area becomes commercial, and the restriction no longer provides real benefit.

blocks.text: A neighborhood restriction says only single-family homes.
Decades later, the entire area becomes commercial, and the restriction no longer provides real benefit.

sections.title: 126. Common-Interest Communities
body: A common-interest community has individual owners plus shared obligations.
Owners usually must pay assessments even if they do not use the amenities.
HOA/condo = private government for the property community.

blocks.text: A common-interest community has individual owners plus shared obligations.
Owners usually must pay assessments even if they do not use the amenities.
HOA/condo = private government for the property community.

sections.title: 127. Fixtures
body: A fixture is personal property attached to land so it becomes treated as real property.
A built-in kitchen cabinet is likely a fixture.
Fixture = personal property turned into real property.

blocks.text: A fixture is personal property attached to land so it becomes treated as real property.
A built-in kitchen cabinet is likely a fixture.
Fixture = personal property turned into real property.

sections.title: 128. Trade Fixtures
body: A trade fixture is installed by a commercial tenant for business use.
A tenant may usually remove trade fixtures if removal happens on time and does not cause serious damage.
Leo leases a storefront and installs display shelving for his clothing shop.
He may usually remove it before the lease ends if he repairs damage.
Trade fixture belongs to tenant if removed properly.

blocks.text: A trade fixture is installed by a commercial tenant for business use.
A tenant may usually remove trade fixtures if removal happens on time and does not cause serious damage.
Leo leases a storefront and installs display shelving for his clothing shop.
He may usually remove it before the lease ends if he repairs damage.
Trade fixture belongs to tenant if removed properly.

sections.title: 129. Crops and Emblements
body: If a tenant with uncertain lease duration plants crops and the lease ends through no fault of the tenant, the tenant may reenter to harvest.
Leo rents farmland for as long as Maya wants.
Maya suddenly ends the tenancy before harvest.
Emblements protect tenant-grown crops when tenancy ends unexpectedly.

blocks.text: If a tenant with uncertain lease duration plants crops and the lease ends through no fault of the tenant, the tenant may reenter to harvest.
Leo rents farmland for as long as Maya wants.
Maya suddenly ends the tenancy before harvest.
Emblements protect tenant-grown crops when tenancy ends unexpectedly.

sections.title: 131. Nonconforming Use
body: A nonconforming use existed legally before zoning changed.
Leo’s bakery existed before the area was rezoned residential.
Grandfathered use may continue, but usually cannot grow.

blocks.text: A nonconforming use existed legally before zoning changed.
Leo’s bakery existed before the area was rezoned residential.
Grandfathered use may continue, but usually cannot grow.

sections.title: 132. Variance
body: A variance lets an owner use land in a way zoning normally forbids.
Allows a different kind of use.
Hardship must come from the property, not just the owner’s finances.
Variance = special permission because property is unusual.

blocks.text: A variance lets an owner use land in a way zoning normally forbids.
Allows a different kind of use.
Hardship must come from the property, not just the owner’s finances.
Variance = special permission because property is unusual.

sections.title: 133. Special Exception Permit
body: The zoning ordinance itself allows certain uses if conditions are met.
A residential zone allows daycare centers with a special permit.
Maya applies and satisfies the conditions.
Special exception = ordinance says “maybe, if conditions met.”

blocks.text: The zoning ordinance itself allows certain uses if conditions are met.
A residential zone allows daycare centers with a special permit.
Maya applies and satisfies the conditions.
Special exception = ordinance says “maybe, if conditions met.”

sections.title: 134. Spot Zoning
body: Spot zoning is arbitrary rezoning of a small parcel inconsistent with surrounding zoning and the comprehensive plan.
One residential lot is rezoned for a large factory only to benefit one owner.
That may be illegal spot zoning.
Spot zoning = unfair special treatment for one parcel.

blocks.text: Spot zoning is arbitrary rezoning of a small parcel inconsistent with surrounding zoning and the comprehensive plan.
One residential lot is rezoned for a large factory only to benefit one owner.
That may be illegal spot zoning.
Spot zoning = unfair special treatment for one parcel.

sections.title: 135. Dedication
body: Dedication means giving land for public use.
Owner offers land for public use.
Acceptance can be express or implied by public use.
A developer marks a walkway on a subdivision map for public use, and the city accepts it.
Dedication = private owner gives public use rights.

blocks.text: Dedication means giving land for public use.
Owner offers land for public use.
Acceptance can be express or implied by public use.
A developer marks a walkway on a subdivision map for public use, and the city accepts it.
Dedication = private owner gives public use rights.

sections.title: 136. Nuisance
body: A private nuisance is a substantial and unreasonable interference with someone’s use and enjoyment of land.
A public nuisance is an unreasonable interference with rights common to the public.
A private plaintiff must show special harm different in kind from the public.
A factory releases odors that make nearby homes unusable.
If the odor affects an entire public park, public nuisance may also be possible.
Private nuisance = my land enjoyment. Public nuisance = community rights.

blocks.text: A private nuisance is a substantial and unreasonable interference with someone’s use and enjoyment of land.
A public nuisance is an unreasonable interference with rights common to the public.
A private plaintiff must show special harm different in kind from the public.
A factory releases odors that make nearby homes unusable.
If the odor affects an entire public park, public nuisance may also be possible.
Private nuisance = my land enjoyment. Public nuisance = community rights.

sections.title: 137. Water Rights
body: Landowners bordering a watercourse may make reasonable use of the water.
The first person to make beneficial use has priority.
Water rights depend heavily on the jurisdiction.

blocks.text: Landowners bordering a watercourse may make reasonable use of the water.
The first person to make beneficial use has priority.
Water rights depend heavily on the jurisdiction.

sections.title: 138. Support Rights
body: A landowner has the right to support from neighboring land.
If a neighbor excavates and your land collapses in its natural state, the neighbor is strictly liable.
If your building contributed to the collapse, you usually need negligence.
Maya digs deeply near Leo’s undeveloped land, and Leo’s soil collapses.
Subjacent support means support from underneath.
It often arises with mineral rights.
Natural land support gets strong protection.

blocks.text: A landowner has the right to support from neighboring land.
If a neighbor excavates and your land collapses in its natural state, the neighbor is strictly liable.
If your building contributed to the collapse, you usually need negligence.
Maya digs deeply near Leo’s undeveloped land, and Leo’s soil collapses.
Subjacent support means support from underneath.
It often arises with mineral rights.
Natural land support gets strong protection.

sections.title: 139. Air Rights
body: A landowner has reasonable use of airspace above the land.
No General Right to View or Sunlight
A neighbor can usually build in a way that blocks sunlight or a view unless acting only out of spite or violating a specific right.
Maya builds a lawful second story that blocks Leo’s scenic view.
Leo usually has no claim merely because the view is blocked.
You own reasonable airspace, not guaranteed sunlight or view.

blocks.text: A landowner has reasonable use of airspace above the land.
No General Right to View or Sunlight
A neighbor can usually build in a way that blocks sunlight or a view unless acting only out of spite or violating a specific right.
Maya builds a lawful second story that blocks Leo’s scenic view.
Leo usually has no claim merely because the view is blocked.
You own reasonable airspace, not guaranteed sunlight or view.

sections.title: Final Real Property MBE Attack Plan
body: Step 2: Identify the Type of Interest
Real property questions usually turn on roles.
Step 4: Ask What Remedy Is Sought
Executory interest = third party gets it
Marketable title = no unreasonable litigation risk
Notice statute = later BFP without notice wins
Race statute = first to record wins
Race-notice = no notice + first to record
Fixture = attached chattel becomes real property

blocks.text: Step 2: Identify the Type of Interest
Real property questions usually turn on roles.
Step 4: Ask What Remedy Is Sought
Executory interest = third party gets it
Marketable title = no unreasonable litigation risk
Notice statute = later BFP without notice wins
Race statute = first to record wins
Race-notice = no notice + first to record
Fixture = attached chattel becomes real property

sections.title: Final Memory Sheet
body: Fee simple subject to condition subsequent = grantor must act
Fee simple subject to executory interest = third party takes automatically
Life estate = ownership for a lifetime
Waste = do not ruin property for future owner
Reversion = back to grantor after life estate
Possibility of reverter = grantor after determinable fee
Right of reentry = grantor after condition subsequent
Remainder = third party after natural end of prior estate
Executory interest = third party cuts short estate
RAP = must vest or fail within life in being plus 21 years
Will does not sever joint tenancy
Tenancy at will = either side can end
Constructive eviction = substantial interference + notice + no fix + leave
Part performance can rescue oral contract.
Marketable title means no serious title defects.
Time is not essential unless stated.
Merger means after closing, look to the deed.
Equitable conversion means buyer owns in equity after contract.
Deed needs grantor, grantee, transfer words, property description, and grantor signature.
Delivery means present intent to transfer.
Notice statute means no notice wins.
Race statute means first to record wins.
Race-notice means no notice and first to record.
Wild deed means recorded outside the chain.
Subject to = buyer not personally liable
Equity of redemption = before foreclosure sale
Foreclosure wipes out juniors, not seniors
Implied reciprocal servitude = subdivision common plan
Fixture = attached personal property becomes real property
Trade fixture = tenant business item removable if done properly
Private nuisance = interference with land enjoyment
Public nuisance = interference with public right
